claims.claim: The overall capacity degradation hierarchy across all five protocols was: 4C CC–CV 15 min > 4C CC–CV 60 %SOC > 3C CC > 2C CC > 4C CC.
claims.quote: The overall degradation hierarchy was: 4C CC–CV 15 min > 4C CC–CV 60 %SOC > 3C CC > 2C CC > 4C CC.

claims.claim: Under 4C CC–CV 15 min cycling, capacity fade is minimal during early cycles but degrades rapidly in later cycles, reaching 80% SOH first among all protocols tested.
claims.quote: cells charged at 4C CC–CV 15 min exhibited minimal fade during early cycles but degraded rapidly in later cycles, reaching 80 % SOH first.

claims.claim: Under 3C CC and 4C CC cycling, impedance and capacity degrade rapidly at first but then plateau or slow in the mid-to-late stages as the SEI stabilizes.
claims.quote: At 3C CC and 4C CC, impedance and capacity degrade rapidly at first but then plateau or slow in the mid-to-late stages as the SEI stabilizes.

claims.claim: Under 2C CC cycling, impedance and capacity loss worsen continuously without stabilization.
claims.quote: Under 2C CC cycling, impedance and capacity loss worsen continuously.

claims.claim: Peak cell temperature stays below 50°C across all five charging protocols tested.
claims.quote: the peak cell temperature stays below 50 ◦C across all charging protocols.

claims.claim: The heating coil method triggered thermal runaway after 225 s of heating, which was 34.00 s and 57.79 s faster than the heating rod and heating plate methods, respectively, under identical 265 W heating power.
claims.quote: thermal runaway is triggered after 225 s of heating with the coil, reaching 140.07 C higher than the temperatures triggered by the rod and plate, respectively. Triggering times are reduced by 34.00 s and 57.79 s, respectively.

claims.claim: Under the heating coil method, the battery peak temperature after thermal runaway was the lowest at 326.03 °C, while the heating rod produced the highest peak temperature at 508.67 °C, and the heating plate produced an intermediate peak temperature of 460.35 °C.
claims.quote: The peak temperature data indicate the following order from lowest to highest: heating coil (326.03 C), heating plate (460.35 C), and heating rod (508.67 C).

claims.claim: Under heating coil conditions, the peak chamber pressure was the highest at 430.43 kPa despite the lowest ambient temperature among the three methods, indicating that higher gas generation rate was the primary driver of pressure increase.
claims.quote: under heating coil conditions, the ambient temperature is the lowest, yet the corresponding peak pressure is the highest at 430.43 kPa. This indicates that a higher gas generation rate is the primary driver of the pressure increase.

claims.claim: The heating coil method produced the highest peak gas generation at 7.72 mol, significantly exceeding the heating plate and rod methods, with all methods stabilizing between 1.7 and 2 mol after the peak.
claims.quote: The heating coil method shows the highest peak gas generation at 7.72 mol, significantly exceeding the heating plate and rod methods. Comparing peak gas generation from different heating methods with the corresponding peak pressure, peak temperature, and ambient temperature in Fig. 6 reveals a clear relationship. Peak pressure and ambient temperature rise with increased gas generation, while peak temperature after thermal runaway decreases.

claims.claim: The heating rod method produced the widest flammable concentration range (51.11%) and the lowest lower flammability limit (7.97%) among the three heating methods, making its released mixed gases the most dangerous.
claims.quote: the flammable concentration ranges from largest to smallest for the three heating methods is: heating rod (51.11 %), heating coil (49.82 %), and heating plate (46.94 %). Furthermore, the lower flammability limit is the lowest for the heating rod (7.97 %), followed by the heating plate (8.26 %) and the heating coil (8.88 %).

claims.claim: Under the same 100% SOC conditions, CO2 concentration in thermal runaway gases decreased in the order: heating coil (27.73%), heating plate (24.6%), and heating rod (18.08%), with H2 concentration showing the inverse order: heating rod (25.06%), heating plate (24.33%), heating coil (23.61%).
claims.quote: the descending order of CO2 gas concentrations is as follows: heating coil (27.73 %), heating plate (24.6 %), and heating rod (18.08 %). In contrast to the changes in CO2 gas concentration, the descending order of H2 gas concentrations from high to low is heating rod (25.06 %), heating plate (24.33 %), and heating coil (23.61 %).

claims.claim: Cell safety does not directly connect to non-flammability of electrolytes based on the thermal runaway mechanism.
claims.quote: Cell safety does not directly connect to non-flammability of electrolytes based on the thermal runaway mechanism.

claims.claim: Due to the lack of carbonate structure, ionic liquids produce much less CO2 compared to conventional carbonate electrolytes, which might be an advantage for cell safety.
claims.quote: Due to the lack of carbonate structure, ionic liquids produce much less CO2, which might be an advantage of ionic liquids for the cell safety.

claims.claim: Small exothermic peaks starting at about 120°C in lithium-ion cells can be ascribed to the chemical reduction of ionic liquids by LixC6 anode when partial destruction of the SEI film occurs.
claims.quote: Small exothermic peaks started at about 120oC can be ascribed to the chemical reduction of ionic liquids by LixC6 anode, when the partial destruction of the SEI film occurs.

claims.claim: The chemical oxidation of ionic liquid by LiyNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 cathode began at about 220°C, with more violent heat generation occurring around 280°C due to combustion reactions of electrolyte and separator based on released oxygen from the cathode.
claims.quote: The chemical oxidation of ionic liquid by LiyNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2 cathode began at about 220°C and more violent heat generation occurred around 280°C. The first exothermic reaction is similar to that on the anode, however, the second one was more vigorous due to the combustion reactions of electrolyte and separator based on the released oxygen from the cathode.

claims.claim: Inorganic anions in ionic liquid electrolytes made the onset temperatures of exothermic reactions lower and total enthalpies larger compared to organic anion (TFSI) electrolytes.
claims.quote: Inorganic anions made the onset temperatures lower and total enthalpies larger.
